posts.cooked:
i have heard conflicting opinions about two recent vintages in Burgundy and was wondering about your opinions. From what I heard 2015 are wonderful but 2014 are more ageworthy and might turn out better down the line( although some of my friends disagree with that assement).

I haven’t tried any so can’t really comment myself.

What’s everyone’s take?

Thanks!

JF

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked: Reds or whites?

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
For white, 2014 is a fabulous vintage. I have not had as many 2015 whites as 2014 whites, but the 2015 whites that I have had seem softer and I have not liked them quite as much as the 2014 whites, which I truly love.

Again, I have had more 2014 reds than 2015 reds. The 2015 reds are very rich and impressive. The ones I have tasted I had about a year ago when I was in Burgundy. A friend who has tasted there more recently told me that the wines have become a lot more tannic in the last year, although they are still very rich. The 2014 reds are in a much more elegant style. They have beautiful acidity and are to me similar in style to the 2010 reds, although not as good as the 2010 reds.

I would be very surprised if wines from both vintages do not age very well, but I would expect them to always be very different in style. Most red Burgundies from good producers age well except in fairly bad vintages. I am not sure why people would think that either vintage would not age.

+1, good comments Howard
2014 is a lot like 1985, very pretty straight out of the gate but may suprise you with the longevity of some of the wines, good village wines will need around 12 years to be at their best, premier cru around 15, and Grand Cru around 20.
2015 is a different beast and Im sure this vintage will shut down hard in bottle and take a long time to come around, but will have the tannic depth and ripeness to produce some mindbending wines when fully mature, some of the great wines from this vintage might need 30-40 years to really show what they have got.

Marcus Dean:

posts.name: Howard Davis
posts.cooked:
JF, I tasted 2014s in barrel then bottle on visits to Burgundy, and at tastings since. For reds and whites it is my favourite vintage since 2010 and the largest vintage in my cellar since then. 2014 reds have an elegance and structure which, for many I think, will make them both relatively accessible earlier and relatively long lived.

When I tasted 2015 reds relatively early in barrel I worried about overripeness. However, I’ve since been convinced about the quality of the vintage by friends whose palates I trust, some wines I’ve tasted and the critics favourable views. I think the vintage will be as Marcus describes, one likely to shut down in the short term, that will reward long term cellaring and be very long lived.

I think your approach of buying some wines from each vintage is a wise one.

The Burgundy houses I follow rarely miss on a vintage. For me the concept of great vintages is overrated and the interest is following the makers and climats I like through the different vintages, enjoying the differences.

posts.name: Craig Gleason

posts.name: David Bueker

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked:
JFPelletier:

…From what I heard 2015 are wonderful but 2014 are more ageworthy and might turn out better down the line…
JF

For those of us who have followed Burgundy for a few decades, this is the standard song. If a vintage has great weather, ripe and easy picking, if the wines taste great from barrel, are rich and have real fruit in them, they will be derided as an ‘early maturing, no aging potential’ vintage. If a vintage has tougher weather, lots of table sorting, some unripe fruit, if the wines are acidic and hard at the barrel tastings, they will be hailed as ‘classic burgundy, built for aging’.

Do not be misled. Buy the tasty wine.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
I thought the standard song was:
producer > vineyard > vintage.
Every vintage has its strengths and weaknesses, all interesting.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
JFPelletier:

i have heard conflicting opinions about two recent vintages in Burgundy and was wondering about your opinions. From what I heard 2015 are wonderful but 2014 are more ageworthy and might turn out better down the line( although some of my friends disagree with that assement).

Regarding the reds:
I disagree with what you´ve heard: 2014 is an excellent vintage, the wines are balanced with good concentration and nice fruit, but they will certainly (in general) not be on the level of the outstanding 2015s … ´15 will also be much more ageworthy (and in need of bottle age) than ´14 … on the other hand most of the better ´15s will go through a longer period of being shut-down (very rough estimate, depending on many things, but maybe 2018/19 until 2025/27+) … so for sooner drinking it´s good to have some 2014s … (or older vintages), but the ´14 will also age very well …
Personally I won´t open both vintages (except our annual vintage tasting) before 2026/27 …

In white it´s the other way round …

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: I’m not sure worrying about whether the wines will age is really a consideration that anyone should have. There is not a vintage from the last 25 years that has fallen over (and that includes '92 which are really delicious right now). 2014 and 2015 are both very good vintages, in fact I reckon '15 is a great vintage, In general the '14’s are generous and sweet with nice fruit and a line of minerally acidity. There is something cool and floral to the '14’s. The '14’s drink beautifully young and I reckon it is a better vintage for red than '07 and '11. '15’s are deep, powerful, with wonderful flesh and a line of tartaric acidity giving backbone. You may not notice the structures at the moment as there’s waves of luscious fruit but its there.

Yes, I could do it (almost) without any 2004s and 1994s … but I bought conservatively in 2004 … and my 1994 are almost gone … so no problem.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Comments here are for both reds and whites, but from my conclusions from my trip there in May, hope they are helpful:

2015—also difficult to assess for reds in that a lot of them are 1-3 months in bottle so still may be in shock-and-lock stage. Although there was a spread and I had trouble nailing it down, I probably in the end would agree with winemaker Vincent Bottreau at Domaine D’Ardhuy—09 with more acidity. The Clos De Beze from Marchand-Tawse was easily the best red Burg of the trip. The whites I tried show a surprising level of sophistication and lovely mouthfeel this early—I may be buying.

2014—the reds right now are showing primal and intense. I think most are also showing a clarity of terroir, with Pommard in particular being one to chase for this. Taupenot-Merme’s MSD La Riotte was also very notable. Time in bottle really needed to fully assess, but I think the accolades are more or less deserved. The whites, from Domaine Dublere’s Chablis Valmur to Domaine D’Ardhuy’s Corton Charlemagne, continue to be tremendous.

JF

This is a golden age for Burgundy vintages. Was not always this way. Somehow I have not yet had any 2013s, so I cannot comment on those. But, otherwise, since 1999, I would avoid 2004 like the plague, be careful on buying 2003s (either great or weird in my experience) and maybe stay away from many 2011s (although not sure yet) and then you can probably buy from any other vintage as long as you like the producer and the style of the vintage.

I would say that you should really focus on learning the producers you like. That should be your first and most important guide to allocating your financial resources. Don’t just buy highly rated producers without tasting them. Styles of different top level producers in Burgundy can vary widely and you will like the wines of some producers more than those of other producers. Get together a group and do a wine tasting to find which ones you like. Or buy their Bourgogne Rouge. But find out.

Once you hit producers you like, you can often control pricing by going up or down in vineyards. My Burgundy group limits our tasting once a year or so to villages level wines and I must admit that every year I wonder why I spend up for Grand Crus and Premier Crus. The villages level wines from the better producers can be really good.

posts.name: R Nanda

Bumping this up.

Now that 2015s are starting to hit U.S. shores, what buying strategy are people taking on 2015 vis a vis 2014s that might still be available in the system?

I’m loading up on all my favorites in 2015 and also doubling down on some 2014s that now look more attractive on a relative price basis. As a result, I could end up relatively long on 2014, which I’m okay with.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
I’m buying village wines from top producers (Dujac, Fourrier, Rouget) in 2015 for early pleasure-bomb drinking. And buying 1er crus in 2014 from producers who I think are aligned with the vintage style wise (Montille, Bertheau, Barthod).

But what do I know?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
While there are certainly plenty of lovely 2014s, and it is a vintage I purchased for my own cellar, re-tasting them extensively from bottle (250 or so this year) has been a little surprising, as the succulent fruit and classical profile that made them so appealing from barrel is, in some cases, giving way to a tannic structure which is both more assertive and less phenolically mature than expected. Several winemaker friends have seconded this perception. And I begin to wonder whether the comparatively short 2014 ripening season (90 days vs a traditional 100) will ultimately express itself in tannins which are a bit less ripe.

By contrast, 2013s, which often seemed a bit meager and angular from barrel, are now often filling out nicely, and this is actually the vintage where I would venture that careful shoppers can find bargains. Interestingly, 2013 was a protracted season which, according to Christophe Roumier, corresponds very closely in terms of the raw data to 1978 (cool, with 110 days of hang-time). At first glance the comparison isn’t obvious, but it may well be that in the last decade we have forgotten how to taste vintages like 1978 from barrel… But in any case, I am actively looking to taste and purchase more 2013s.

As for 2015s, the serious producers really didn’t produce near-term wines, and many are already beginning to shut down. Anyone who thinks that 2015 has anything in common with 2009 and purchases them for near-term pleasure is likely to be sorely disappointed. It depends on the style of the domaine, of course, but even e.g. Mugneret-Gibourg’s Bourgogne rouge will clearly be better at age 15 than it is at age 10. Many of the higher appellation wines currently taste a bit inchoate and very primary, even if their mid-palates are sufficiently ample to conceal their prodigious tannins for the moment. If you do catch them when they’re open for inspection, of course, they will be ‘pleasure bombs’ - but I don’t think they will be delivering an appreciably different experience to a luscious 2015 cru Beaujolais, whereas the tariff will be considerably higher; you will be tasting the sunshine and the fruit, but not really what you paid for.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
Thanks, William. Interesting thoughts and helpful insights on all three vintages.

2013s could indeed turn out to be quite the bargain. Any particular villages or producers to look out for in 2013?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Rich Nanda:

Thanks, William. Interesting thoughts and helpful insights on all three vintages.

2013s could indeed turn out to be quite the bargain. Any particular villages or producers to look out for in 2013?

Well, it’s definitely a vintage to parse by producer. But at the village level, I have loved Domaine Pierre Guillemot’s Savigny VV, Berthaut’s Fixin Les Crais, and both Roumier’s and Barthod’s Chambolle AOC. Liger-Belair’s Nuits Aux Cras is excellent and was delicious from the beginning, and the Georges Noellat Nuits Boudots is also quite precociously attractive, maybe even at its best now. I have not opened many higher appellation bottles, but intend to make a concerted effort to taste more, professionally and personally, in 2018.

That’s really interesting William. I didn’t taste 2013 reds from barrel but what I tasted from bottle on release wasn’t my favorite and I made only limited purchases. Your post has me inclined to revisit the wines.

I’m not buying much of 2015 either, but that’s for the opposite reason - the wines I’ve tasted have been quite good (and as others have said, do not taste particularly tannic), but the prices so far have reflected the hype which has limited my buying.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
JLee:

That’s really interesting William. I didn’t taste 2013 reds from barrel but what I tasted from bottle on release wasn’t my favorite and I made only limited purchases. Your post has me inclined to revisit the wines.

I’m not buying much of 2015 either, but that’s for the opposite reason - the wines I’ve tasted have been quite good (and as others have said, do not taste particularly tannic), but the prices so far have reflected the hype which has limited my buying.

I would say that it’s definitely worth looking at your favorite producers, and if you can try a bottle before loading up, so much the better. Unless the market balks at 2015 pricing and the wines end up getting discounted (which I don’t see happening), 2013 might conceivably be the last relatively ‘cheap’ red Burgundy vintage, in the way that 2008 was in Bordeaux.

As far as 2015s are concerned, fortunately there are plenty of lower-appellation wines which are quite exciting and not too expensive which will be hitting the market soon: Volnay and Pommard villages from Glantenay and Voillot, Beaune 1er crus from Drouhin and Jadot, Auxey reds from H & G Lafouge, Maranges 1er Crus Reds from Bachelet-Monnot, Marsannays from Audoin, to name just a few that come to mind.

Very useful post overall, thanks William. I found myself liking 13s more than most people, and did a lot of careful shopping as you say. Particularly some of the domaines which tend toward fleshier and riper wines anyway, where 2013 held them naturally in check.

2013 might conceivably be the last relatively ‘cheap’ red Burgundy vintage, in the way that 2008 was in Bordeaux.

Except then 2014 happened in Bordeaux.

I have lost track of how many times people have proclaimed the end of affordability or the end of relatively cheap wine.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:
William Kelley:

2013 might conceivably be the last relatively ‘cheap’ red Burgundy vintage, in the way that 2008 was in Bordeaux.

Except then 2014 happened in Bordeaux.

I have lost track of how many times people have proclaimed the end of affordability or the end of relatively cheap wine.

So true, and 2014 deals keep popping up. I suspect this vintage is getting a little lost in the hype of 15 and 16.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:
D@vid Bu3ker:
William Kelley:

2013 might conceivably be the last relatively ‘cheap’ red Burgundy vintage, in the way that 2008 was in Bordeaux.

Except then 2014 happened in Bordeaux.

I have lost track of how many times people have proclaimed the end of affordability or the end of relatively cheap wine.

So true, and 2014 deals keep popping up. I suspect this vintage is getting a little lost in the hype of 15 and 16.

Well, up to a point. A 12-bottle case of 2014 Calon Ségur trades for 800 GBP; I bought cases of the 2008 for 250. Looking at a few other wines which I remember from the EP campaign, the story is the same, more or less, for plenty of them, e.g. Canon, Talbot, GPL - to say nothing of the first growths.

But that’s incidental, really. The Burgundy market is very different because there is so much less wine.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Until people move on to something else.

Robert Alfert:
D@vid Bu3ker:
William Kelley:

2013 might conceivably be the last relatively ‘cheap’ red Burgundy vintage, in the way that 2008 was in Bordeaux.

Except then 2014 happened in Bordeaux.

I have lost track of how many times people have proclaimed the end of affordability or the end of relatively cheap wine.

So true, and 2014 deals keep popping up. I suspect this vintage is getting a little lost in the hype of 15 and 16.

In North America, I see more deals on 2013 than 2014 and 2015. The bargains are right now on the 2013; I am backfilling 2013 Cote de Nuits, not so much Cote de Beaune which is below in quality in my (modest) opinion.
